items.q: How do you say 'I like coffee' in French?
items.why: English drops articles for general likes/dislikes; French keeps them. For general preferences, use the DEFINITE article: j'aime le chocolat, je déteste les épinards.

items.q: When do you use the imperfect rather than the passé composé?
items.correct: For ongoing, habitual, or descriptive past actions.
items.random_wrong: When you don't know what tense to use.
items.why: Students confuse past tenses by recency. The distinction is ASPECTUAL: passé composé = completed event ('I went'); imparfait = ongoing/habitual/descriptive ('I used to go', 'I was going').

items.q: What does 'mes parents' mean in French?
items.correct: My parents (mother and father specifically) - but 'parents' can also mean relatives more broadly.
items.misconception: It always means all my relatives.
items.why: Students think French 'parents' = English 'relatives' because in some contexts it does. Usually 'mes parents' = mother+father; 'mes proches' or 'ma famille' = relatives more broadly. Context matters.

items.q: Which sentence means 'We should recycle more'?
items.why: Correct answer: On devrait recycler plus.. “On devra recycler plus.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: global warming. “recycling” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many of the famous 'three Rs' of environmentalism are there (réduire, réutiliser, recycler)?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 Rs. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
